items.q: How does fertiliser runoff cause aquatic dead zones?
items.correct: Excess nutrients fuel algal blooms; when algae die, decomposition depletes water oxygen.
items.random_wrong: Fertiliser makes the water too acidic.
items.why: Students assume direct toxicity. Eutrophication is INDIRECT - nutrients cause excess plant growth, then decomposers consume oxygen breaking it down, suffocating fish and other aerobic organisms.

items.q: How are most microplastics formed in the environment?
items.correct: Larger plastic items break down physically into smaller fragments over time.
items.misconception: They are all manufactured at microscopic size deliberately.
items.why: Students think microplastics are designed small. Most are SECONDARY - formed when sun, waves, and wear break down larger items. Primary microplastics (microbeads, fibres) are a smaller proportion.

items.q: How do greenhouse gases warm Earth?
items.correct: They absorb infrared radiation emitted by Earth's surface and re-radiate some back down.
items.misconception: They block heat from escaping into space (like a literal lid).
items.random_wrong: They reflect sunlight back to Earth.
items.why: Students picture a physical blanket. The mechanism is selective absorption - greenhouse gases let visible light pass IN but absorb outgoing infrared. The re-emission warms the lower atmosphere and surface.

items.q: Which units commonly express water pollutant concentration?
items.why: Correct answer: mg/L. “kg/m” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: High BOD in a river indicates what?
items.why: Correct answer: Organic pollution. “Low temperature” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: 100 mg of pollutant in 20 L water gives what concentration in mg/L?
items.why: Exact answer: 5. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
